name: Caladrius
description: The Caladrius is a mythical bird, pure white in color, found in Roman and medieval bestiaries. It is said to possess the ability to predict whether a sick person will live or die. According to legend, if the Caladrius looks at the sick person, it signifies that the person will recover. The bird is believed to absorb the illness, subsequently flying away and dispersing the disease into the sun, thereby curing the patient. However, if the Caladrius turns its gaze away from the sick person, it indicates that the person is destined to die, and the bird refuses to take on their ailment. The Caladrius's existence is largely symbolic, representing Christ's willingness to take on the sins and suffering of humanity. Its association with healing and prophecy made it a popular figure in medieval art and literature, often depicted in illuminated manuscripts and bestiaries as a symbol of divine grace and the power of discernment.
subtitle: A divinely white bird that predicts sickness and health.
appearance: The Caladrius is described as a pure white bird, often compared to a dove or swan in its appearance. It is generally depicted as being of moderate size, with a gentle and serene demeanor. Its plumage is immaculate, representing its purity and association with the divine. Some accounts suggest that its eyes possess a unique clarity, allowing it to perceive the state of a person's health with uncanny accuracy.
habitat: Royal courts and palaces, particularly those of kings and emperors, as the Caladrius was often kept as a prized possession due to its prognostic abilities. It is also sometimes associated with sacred groves or temples.
behavior: The Caladrius is said to be a discerning creature, carefully observing the sick before making its judgment. Its behavior is described as deliberate and purposeful, with its gaze indicating its decision about the patient's fate. When it looks at a sick person, it displays a sense of empathy and willingness to take on their illness. Conversely, when it turns away, it demonstrates a detachment and refusal to intervene, signifying the patient's impending demise.
weaknesses: Inability to heal those destined to die
Dependent on human interaction for purpose

keyMyths.title: The Caladrius and the Emperor's Illness
keyMyths.description: An emperor falls gravely ill, and all hope seems lost. The court brings in a Caladrius, which looks upon the emperor with kindness. Soon after, the emperor begins to recover, proving the bird's ability to absorb illness and signaling the emperor's destined recovery. The court rejoices at the bird's benevolent nature.

keyMyths.title: The Refusal of the Caladrius
keyMyths.description: A condemned prisoner lies on their deathbed, plagued by a mysterious disease. The Caladrius is brought before them, but the bird refuses to look at the prisoner, turning its head away. This act confirms the prisoner's impending death, highlighting the Caladrius's role not just as a healer but also as a harbinger of fate.

keyMyths.title: The Caladrius as a Symbol of Christ
keyMyths.description: Christian scholars interpret the Caladrius's willingness to take on illness as a parallel to Christ's sacrifice for humanity. Just as the Caladrius absorbs disease, Christ took on the sins of the world. This interpretation solidified the Caladrius's place in Christian symbolism and art.

sources.author: T.H. White
sources.work: The Book of Beasts: Being a Translation from a Latin Bestiary of the Twelfth Century
sources.description: A comprehensive translation and analysis of a medieval bestiary, providing detailed descriptions of various mythical creatures, including the Caladrius, and their symbolic meanings.
sources.text: A comprehensive translation and analysis of a medieval bestiary, providing detailed descriptions of various mythical creatures, including the Caladrius, and their symbolic meanings.

sources.author: Isidore of Seville
sources.description: A seminal work of encyclopedic knowledge from the early Middle Ages, containing descriptions of animals, including the Caladrius, and their allegorical interpretations within a Christian context.
sources.text: A seminal work of encyclopedic knowledge from the early Middle Ages, containing descriptions of animals, including the Caladrius, and their allegorical interpretations within a Christian context.

sources.author: Florence McCulloch
sources.description: An in-depth study of medieval bestiaries, exploring the origins, evolution, and significance of animal symbolism in medieval literature and art, with specific attention to the Caladrius.
sources.text: An in-depth study of medieval bestiaries, exploring the origins, evolution, and significance of animal symbolism in medieval literature and art, with specific attention to the Caladrius.
